title: SEGMENT 2 — FDA, CBER, OTAT — the structure of the US gene therapy regulator

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — FDA, CBER, OTAT — the structure of the US gene therapy regulator
Let me orient you geographically, organizationally, within the agency that approves gene therapies in the United States.
Because once you understand the structure, you will understand a great deal about why certain decisions get made the way they do.
The Food and Drug Administration — the FDA — is a federal agency under the Department of Health and Human Services, headquartered in Silver Spring, Maryland.
Its mandate is to regulate, among other things, all drugs, all biologics, all medical devices, and most food sold in the United States.
It is one of the oldest consumer protection agencies in the world; its lineage goes back to the late nineteenth century.
Today it employs roughly eighteen thousand people across multiple centers, and it reviews — in any given year — thousands of drug and biologic applications.

For our purposes, the relevant subdivision is called the Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research, or CBER — pronounced "see-ber," not "sea-bear," and you should mostly call it by the acronym because nobody in the field uses the full name.
CBER is the part of FDA that handles biologics — meaning drugs that are derived from living systems rather than synthesized by traditional chemistry.
Vaccines, blood products, allergenic extracts, cell therapies, gene therapies, and tissue products all fall under CBER.
The other major drug center at FDA, by contrast, is CDER — the Center for Drug Evaluation and Research — which handles small-molecule drugs and most monoclonal antibodies.
Gene therapy is regulated as a biologic.
That means CBER, not CDER, is the agency you deal with.

Within CBER, gene therapies have historically been reviewed by an office originally called the Office of Tissues and Advanced Therapies, or OTAT — pronounced "oh-tat" — which until recently was the specific office handling gene and cell therapy applications.

In 2023, CBER reorganized OTAT into a larger structure called the Office of Therapeutic Products, or OTP, reflecting the rapid growth in cell and gene therapy submissions and the need to scale up review capacity.

The functions are essentially continuous — the reviewers and the framework moved more or less wholesale — but if you read older literature you will see "OTAT" and in newer literature you will see "OTP.
" For this lecture I will use both interchangeably, because the work being done is the same.
The reorganization was a recognition that this field is no longer a niche; it is one of the busiest areas of biologics review in the agency.
OTAT or OTP, sitting inside CBER, sitting inside FDA — that is the chain of command you need to know.
When you submit a gene therapy application, your file lands on the desk of a reviewer who sits in this office.

That reviewer is, very often, a person with a PhD in molecular biology or biochemistry, perhaps a clinical degree on top of it, who has spent years developing expertise specifically in how viral vectors and editing systems behave in patients.
These are not generalists.

The depth of expertise inside CBER on, say, AAV serotype selection or the manufacturing of lentiviral vectors is, in many cases, comparable to or better than the expertise inside any individual biotech company.
The agency knows the field.
They are not bureaucrats in a vacuum.
They are a peer-review body with regulatory authority.
Now let me say a word about the European counterpart, because I will keep coming back to it.
The European Medicines Agency, or EMA, is the body that approves drugs across the European Union.
It is headquartered in Amsterdam, having moved there from London after Brexit.

Within the EMA, the committee that specifically handles gene therapy and other "advanced therapy medicinal products" — the EU's umbrella term for cell therapy, gene therapy, and tissue-engineered products — is called the Committee for Advanced Therapies, or CAT — yes, the acronym is CAT, and yes, people in the field make jokes about it.

CAT does the scientific review and writes the recommendation; the recommendation then goes to a higher-level committee called the CHMP, the Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use, which formally adopts the opinion that becomes the basis for the European Commission's marketing authorization.
So in the US, the answer to "who approves a gene therapy" is FDA, via CBER, via OTAT/OTP.
In the EU, the answer is the European Commission, on the advice of CHMP, on the recommendation of CAT, inside EMA.
Different acronyms, different structures, similar functions.
We'll see throughout this lecture that the two agencies usually arrive at similar decisions, but they get there on slightly different schedules and with slightly different emphases.
